questions.question: What is the **default** consistency level for a new Azure Cosmos DB account?
options: Strong
Bounded Staleness
Session
Eventual
questions.explanation: Session is the default. It gives read-your-writes and monotonic reads/writes within a client session at eventual-level cost (1x RU). Strong is not the default; many developers wrongly assume it is.

questions.question: For a brand-new greenfield application, which Cosmos DB API should you choose, and why?
options: MongoDB API, because Mongo drivers are the most mature
NoSQL (Core) API, because new features land there first and it has the richest SDK
Table API, because it is the cheapest to run
Gremlin API, because property graphs are the most flexible model
questions.explanation: NoSQL (Core) is the native API and gets every capability first (change feed, hierarchical keys, vector search, the full SDK). The other APIs exist primarily for migration/compatibility, and the API is immutable after account creation.

questions.question: What is the maximum size of a single Cosmos DB item (JSON document)?
options: 2 MB
20 GB
16 MB
50 GB
questions.explanation: An individual item is capped at 2 MB; large blobs should go to Azure Storage with only the URL stored in Cosmos. 20 GB is the logical-partition storage cap and ~50 GB is a physical partition — different limits.

questions.question: Approximately how many RU does a **point read** of a 1 KB item by `id` and partition key cost?
options: About 1 RU
About 5 RU
About 10 RU
About 0.1 RU
questions.explanation: A 1 KB point read by id costs about 1 RU and is the cheapest, lowest-latency operation. The RU model normalizes a point read of a 1 KB item to roughly 1 RU; queries and writes cost more.

questions.question: Reads under **Strong** and **Bounded Staleness** consistency cost roughly 2x the RU of Session/Consistent-Prefix/Eventual reads because they perform quorum reads.
questions.explanation: Strong and Bounded Staleness guarantee freshness via quorum reads (two replicas of a four-replica set), so they cost about 2x RU. Session, Consistent Prefix, and Eventual read a single replica at 1x RU.

questions.question: A container uses `/country` as its partition key and 80% of traffic is `US`. RU/s is provisioned generously, yet requests return HTTP 429. What is the root cause?
options: The account-level RU/s limit has been exceeded
A low-cardinality, skewed key concentrates traffic on one logical partition, capping you at that partition's RU/s share
The items exceed the 2 MB size limit and are being throttled
Strong consistency is doubling the RU cost of every read
questions.explanation: Provisioned RU/s is split evenly across physical partitions, so a skewed low-cardinality key (`/country`) hashes most traffic to one logical/physical partition — a hot partition — yielding 429s while the container looks under-utilized. The cure is a high-cardinality, evenly-accessed key.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes a **logical** partition from a **physical** partition?
options: A logical partition is the ~50 GB storage node; a physical partition is the 20 GB transaction boundary
A logical partition is all items sharing one partition-key value (the 20 GB transaction boundary); a physical partition is the storage/compute node (~50 GB, ~10k RU/s)
A logical partition and a physical partition are the same thing, named differently per API
A physical partition is capped at 20 GB and you split it manually as data grows
questions.explanation: A logical partition = all items with the same partition-key value, is the transaction boundary, and is capped at 20 GB. A physical partition is the actual node (~50 GB, ~10k RU/s) onto which Cosmos maps many logical partitions and splits automatically.

questions.question: Why would you choose **hierarchical (subpartitioned) partition keys** such as `/tenantId` then `/userId` for a multi-tenant app?
options: To allow changing the partition key in place later without migrating data
To enable cross-partition transactions spanning multiple tenants
To spread a single large tenant across sub-partitions (beating the 20 GB cap) while keeping prefix queries efficient
To remove the need for an indexing policy entirely
questions.explanation: A single huge tenant would blow the 20 GB logical-partition cap on a flat `/tenantId` key; the hierarchy spreads it across sub-partitions while a prefix query (`/tenantId` alone) still targets only matching sub-partitions. The hierarchy is still fixed at container creation, so changing it later requires migration.

questions.question: Traffic to a container is highly variable and spiky with unpredictable peaks, and you want to avoid 429s without permanently over-provisioning. Which capacity mode fits best?
options: Provisioned (manual) at the peak RU/s you ever expect
Autoscale with a max RU/s, scaling instantly between 10% and 100% of that max
Serverless, paying per request
Provisioned (manual) at the average RU/s
questions.explanation: Autoscale lets you set a max RU/s and scales instantly between 10% and 100% based on load, ideal for variable/spiky traffic without over-provisioning. Manual at peak wastes money on idle; manual at average causes 429s on spikes; serverless suits dev/test and low/intermittent volume, not a high spiky production load.

questions.question: You can configure **Strong** consistency on an account that has **multi-region writes** enabled.
questions.explanation: Strong consistency is incompatible with multi-region writes: a multi-write distributed system cannot provide RPO=0/RTO=0. For multi-region write accounts use Session or weaker (Bounded Staleness is also an anti-pattern there).

questions.question: A team uses the **default (latest-version) change feed** to keep an Azure AI Search index in sync. Which behavior must they account for?
options: The change feed surfaces only deletes, not creates or updates
The change feed is unordered, so events can arrive in any sequence within a partition
The default change feed does not surface deletes, so removals need a soft-delete flag or all-versions-and-deletes mode
The change feed requires you to write your own polling loop with no checkpointing
questions.explanation: The default/latest-version change feed records creates and updates ordered per partition but does NOT surface deletes; model a soft-delete flag or use the all-versions-and-deletes mode. The Azure Functions trigger checkpoints automatically, so no manual polling is needed.

questions.question: Which statements about consuming the Cosmos DB **change feed** are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: The Azure Functions Cosmos DB trigger is a push model where the platform polls and invokes your function per batch, checkpointing automatically
The change-feed processor (pull model) uses a lease container to distribute partitions across workers
The change feed is ordered per partition for creates and updates
The change feed guarantees a single global total order across all partitions
The change feed surfaces deletes by default in latest-version mode
questions.explanation: The Functions trigger is push with automatic checkpointing; the change-feed processor is pull and uses a lease container for partition distribution; the feed is ordered per partition. It is not globally totally ordered, and latest-version mode excludes deletes.

questions.question: A write-heavy container stores a large unqueried `/payload` blob, and write RU is high. What is the most effective tuning move?
options: Switch the indexing mode to `none` so nothing is indexed
Exclude `/payload/*` from the indexing policy so it isn't indexed on every write
Add a composite index covering `/payload`
Increase the item size limit beyond 2 MB to fit the payload
questions.explanation: Cosmos auto-indexes every property by default; indexing a large unqueried path inflates write RU and storage. Excluding `/payload/*` cuts that cost while keeping queryable paths indexed. Disabling all indexing would break legitimate queries, and a composite index on an unqueried path adds cost rather than removing it.

questions.question: In this single-partition query helper, what is the purpose of supplying `PartitionKey` in `QueryRequestOptions`?
options: It encrypts the query parameters before sending them
It scopes the query to one logical partition so it doesn't fan out cross-partition
It converts the query into a cheaper point read automatically
It enables Strong consistency for just this query
questions.explanation: Passing the partition key scopes the query to a single logical partition, avoiding a cross-partition fan-out to every physical partition (which costs the most RU and scales worst). It does not turn the query into a point read, nor does it change consistency or add encryption.

questions.question: A .NET API shows rising latency and socket exhaustion under load. The controller does the following per request. What is the fix?
options: Wrap the call in a retry policy to absorb the 429s
Register a single `CosmosClient` as a DI singleton and inject it, instead of constructing one per request
Switch the connection mode from Direct to Gateway to reduce sockets
Add `ConsistencyLevel.Eventual` to the request options
questions.explanation: `CosmosClient` is thread-safe and caches connection pools, the routing/partition map, and (in Direct mode) long-lived TCP connections; constructing one per request exhausts sockets and cold-starts those caches every call. Register one singleton in DI. Retries and Gateway mode don't address the per-request construction; the symptom is not throttling or consistency.

questions.question: Which statement most accurately describes the guarantee of **Bounded Staleness** consistency?
options: Reads always return the latest committed write, exactly like Strong but cheaper
Reads lag by at most K versions or T time, whichever comes first; it is a good fit for multi-region writes
Reads lag by at most K versions or T time; near-strong for single-write-region accounts, an anti-pattern with multi-region writes
Reads may appear out of order but are never more than T seconds stale
questions.explanation: Bounded Staleness bounds lag to at most K versions or T time, giving near-strong behavior across regions for single-write-region accounts; it costs 2x read RU and is an anti-pattern with multi-region writes. It does not guarantee the absolute latest write (that's Strong), and it preserves ordering rather than allowing out-of-order reads.

questions.question: Regarding **conflict resolution** for multi-region (multi-master) writes in Cosmos DB, which statements are true? (Select all that apply.)
options: Last-Writer-Wins (LWW) is the default and uses a numeric/timestamp property to pick a winner
LWW performs a real field-level merge of the two conflicting versions
A custom stored-procedure resolver can run your merge logic, and unresolved conflicts land in a conflicts feed you can inspect
Conflicts can occur when the same item is written concurrently in different regions
Multi-region writes eliminate write conflicts entirely, so no resolution policy is needed
questions.explanation: LWW is the default and picks a winner by a numeric/timestamp property, silently dropping the loser (not a real merge). A custom sproc can merge, and unresolved conflicts go to a conflicts feed. Concurrent same-item writes across regions are exactly what causes conflicts, so a resolution policy is required.

questions.question: You are building RAG over a NoSQL-API container with well over 50,000 vectors per scoped search and need the lowest latency and RU at scale. Which vector index type fits, and what caveat applies?
options: `flat`, because it gives 100% recall and supports up to 4096 dimensions
`diskANN`, an approximate index best above ~50,000 vectors, but it needs at least 1,000 indexed vectors or a full scan runs
`quantizedFlat`, because brute-force is always fastest regardless of vector count
`diskANN`, but it is exact (100% recall) and capped at 505 dimensions
questions.explanation: `diskANN` is the approximate-nearest-neighbor index with the lowest latency/RU and highest throughput at scale (>~50,000 vectors), supporting up to 4096 dimensions, but it needs at least 1,000 indexed vectors or it falls back to a full scan. `flat` is exact but capped at 505 dimensions; `quantizedFlat` suits smaller scoped sets; diskANN is approximate, not exact.
